summary:
Through a series of individual and group activities participating students will reflect on their personal conceptions, attitudes and beliefs regarding culture, and intercultural communication, as a step towards becoming interculturally competent global citizens.
参加学生は、一連の個人およびグループ活動を通して、文化や異文化間コミュニケーションに関する個人的な概念、態度、信念を振り返り、異文化に対応できる国際人になるための一歩を踏み出す。
aims:
The main objective of this course is to develop a greater understanding and empathy for cultures other than one's own, in order to foster global citizenship and harmony.
このコースの主な目的は、グローバル・シチズンシップと調和を育むために、自国以外の文化に対する理解と共感を深めることである。
goals: Students will develop a clear understanding of strategies that can be used to facilitate communication in global contexts
Students will develop a clear understanding of strategies that can be used to facilitate communication in global contexts

plan.text:
Exercise 1 – The culture onion
To enable learners to reflect on their own understanding of culture.
To introduce a model of culture and cultural influences.
To raise awareness of both visible and non-visible components of culture.

Exercise 2 - Cross-cultural checklist
To identify important cultural information that learners may wish to learn before they go overseas.
To illustrate the importance of having a framework for understanding the cultures that learners encounter.

Exercise 3 – Values
To enable learners to identify some of their own values and attitudes.
To provide learners with a vocabulary and model with which to describe cultural influences on workplace behaviour.

Exercise 4 – Recognizing ineffective responses to cultural differences
To develop learners' ability to recognize ineffective individual responses to cross-cultural interactions.
To provide learners with a handy summary of how not to respond to cultural differences on overseas assignments.

Exercise 5 – What is wrong with stereotypes?
To enable learners to reflect on the fact that stereotypes often say more about the individuals that use them, than about the groups that are stereotyped.
To identify particular stereotypes of one culture, and explore how useful they are.

Exercise 6 – What do they need to know about me?
To provide an opportunity for learners to reflect on important elements of their own culture.
To enable learners to prioritize important cultural information about their culture that people from different backgrounds would benefit from knowing.

Exercise 7 – Facts, Attitudes and Behaviours
To provide an opportunity for learners to reflect on key cultural characteristics of another country or culture.

Exercise 8 – Communication types
To enable learners to reflect on some of the typical communication styles commonly encountered in their own and other cultures.
To explore some of the potential misunderstandings that may occur when individuals with different communication styles interact.

Exercise 9 – Hofstede’s dimensions
To enable learners to practice using Hofstede's five-dimension model to explain a number of cross-cultural misunderstandings and cultural differences.

Exercise 10 – Observing body language
To develop learners' ability to attend to the body language of others.
• To enable learners to speculate on the meaning of some important elements of nonverbal communication.

Exercise 11 – Recognising culture shock
To provide clear examples of the various stages associated with culture shock.
To provide learners with practice in identifying stages of culture shock.

Exercise 12 – Culture shock checklist
To summarize useful strategies for managing culture shock and enhancing the process of cross-cultural adjustment.
To provide an opportunity to explore specific behaviours that describe how each strategy can be put into practice.

Exercise 13 – Cross-cultural communication skills checklist
To explore generic strategies for improving cross-cultural communication.
To enable learners to reflect on which strategies may be useful in any given situation

Exercise 14 – What makes a cross-culturally effective individual?
To enable learners to reflect on the characteristics of cross- culturally effective individuals, teams and organizations.
To enable learners to reflect on how well developed their own cross-cultural skills are.

Exercise 15 – Use your RADAR
To explore the five-step 'RADAR' technique for recognizing and overcoming cross-cultural misunderstandings.
To enable learners to practise using this technique to deal with real-life cross-cultural misunderstandings.

textbooks: Materials for this class will be distributed by the instructor
prereq: This course is in particular aimed at students who are planning to study abroad either for short or long terms, participate in overseas volunteer activities or interships, or those simply interested in developing as global citizens. The course is conducted in English so the ability to comprehend and communicate in English is necessary.
prep: Students need to commit sufficient time to out-of-class study to complete set assignments including project work.
teachers: ◎ マイケル シャープ
